text: Ralph De Haas † r ⃝ Victoria Baranov ‡ r ⃝ Ieda Matavelli § r ⃝ Pauline Grosjean ¶ r ⃝ March 21, 2025 Abstract This paper shows how masculinity norms predict decision-making in key socio-economic and political domains.

We collect the first cross-cultural evidence on adherence to masculinity norms among more than 87,000 individuals in 70 countries, combining nationally representative face-to-face surveys and online surveys.

In economics, adherence to dominance masculinity supports labor supply at the intensive margin and competitiveness, but generates frictions by constraining occupational choice to traditionally masculine sectors.
In health, adherence to dominance masculinity is linked to more risk-taking and higher rates of depression.
In politics, it predicts support for anti-democratic and anti-market strongman leadership.

Gender differences in adherence to masculinity norms explain as much as 15% of the gender gap in competitiveness, 30% of the gender gap in the willingness to supply long working hours, 22% of the gender gap in risk aversion, and more than two thirds of the gender gap in support for liberal democracy.
Our results highlight the dual roles of preferences and social norms in explaining our results.

JEL classification: D91; I12; J16; J24; Z13 Keywords: Masculinity; gender norms; gender gaps; occupational sorting; populism * We thank Pablo García Guzmán and Victoria Marino for excellent research assistance.
Our deepest gratitude goes to Cevat Giray Aksoy for insightful feedback and invaluable help with the online component of the Global Masculinity Survey.

We also thank Bocar Ba, Sascha Becker, Jason Barron, Patrick Bayer, Sonia Bhalotra, Leonardo Bursztyn, Raewyn Connell, Adeline Delavande, Erica Field, Moshe Hazan, Ben Golub, Richard Holden, Kristiina Huttunen, Jim Mahalik, Federico Masera, Kostas Matakos, Tim Moore, Paola Profeta, Eva Ranehill, Claudia Senik, Guo Xu, David Yanagizawa-Drott, Jane Zhang, and participants at the Australian Gender Economics Workshop, URPP Conference on Boys and Men (University of Zürich), Econometric Society Australasia Meeting, Duke University, European University Institute, Paris Nanterre University, Monash Centre for Health Economics, National University of Singapore, Berlin Workshop on “Empirical Public Economics: Gender Economics”, ACEA 2024 Conference, and the World Bank conference on “Gender Norms and the Law” for insightful comments.
We are grateful to the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD), the World Bank, the Australian Research Council, the University of Melbourne, and UNSW for financial support.
This project received IRB approval from UNSW (approval number iRECS4759).

The authors’ names have been randomized using a uniform distribution on the American Economic Association website; confirmation code: gKbhnRDIKMfH. † EBRD, KU Leuven and CEPR. E-mail: dehaasr@ebrd.com. ‡ University of Melbourne and CEPR. E-mail: victoria.baranov@unimelb.edu.au. § UNSW. E-mail: i.matavelli@unsw.edu.au. ¶ UNSW and CEPR. E-mail: p.grosjean@unsw.edu.au.
Norms and attitudes about the appropriate roles and behaviors of women play a large role in perpetuating gender inequalities.

They constrain women’s behavior across multiple domains— from sexual and reproductive health and domestic responsibilities, to labor market participation and educational, occupational, and political aspirations. 1 Given their profound impact, gender role norms have drawn wide attention from scholars and media and have driven many policies aimed at promoting gender equality.
In comparison, preferences and social norms about the appropriate behavior of men —in short hand, masculinity norms—have received, until now, less attention.
This paper aims to rectify this imbalance by providing the first large-scale, cross-cultural, and global evidence on individuals’ adherence to masculinity norms.
We show how accounting for masculinity norms deepens our understanding of economic, social, and political decision-making and sheds new light on the drivers of gender inequality.
We measure masculinity norms through survey questions that capture practices identified as characteristic of male behavior.

We focus on ’dominance masculinity’, i.e. behaviors that assert male authority in society and reinforce the subordination of both women and non-conforming men. 2 We extract questions from the Conformity to Masculinity Norms Inventory (CMNI), a standard measure in the social science literature, 3 on 1 We define gender role norms as the socially accepted relative roles of women and men in different spheres of influence, including professional, political, and domestic domains.
For a comprehensive review, see Giuliano; for recent global evidence on (perceptions of) gender role norms, see Bursztyn et al. 2 Dominance masculinity is also referred to as hegemonic masculinity.

Since its initial examination in an ethnographic study of male hierarchies in an Australian high school, the study of dominance masculinity has gained prominence across fields including gender studies, sociology, psychology, and medicine. 3 The CMNI, developed through a qualitative and quantitative process to identify the most prevalent set of norms and expectations characteristic of male behavior (see Section 3. ), is widely used in psychology and public health.

While CMNI scores consistently predict male behavior—particularly in physical and mental health domains —and correlate highly with normative measures of masculinity, most evidence comes from small-scale laboratory studies in developed countries.

five core dimensions of masculinity: the importance of winning, violence, help avoidance, control over women, and disdain for homosexuals, and integrate them into the 2022–2023 Life in Transition Survey (LiTS).

The LiTS is a nationally representative,face-to-face survey conducted by the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) and the World Bank across 44 countries in Europe, Asia, the Middle East and Sub-Saharan Africa that also includes rich modules on socio-demographics, economic conditions, and political attitudes.
We supplement this data with large-scale online surveys in 32 countries in the Americas, Australasia, South and East Asia, Western Europe, and Southern Africa.

Altogether, our Global Masculinity Survey (GMS) includes data from 87,359 respondents in 70 countries across all continents, accounting for almost 70% of the world’s population and 80% of the world’s GDP. 4 The online component provides a focused, short data collection tool to increase our geographical coverage and to collect additional measures that enable us to disentangle the respective roles of preferences and social norms of masculinity.
We first briefly discuss global patterns in adherence to masculinity norms.
The analysis reveals intriguing heterogeneity.
In particular, while Western countries hold far more equal views about gender roles relative to the rest of the world, the divergence is not as stark for dominance masculinity.
Although the levels of adherence to dominance masculinity are lower in Western countries relative to the Middle East, Africa, and Asia, they are on par with former socialist countries of Europe.
There is also much more variation within particular regions in adherence to dominance masculinity compared to attitudes towards gender equality.
The data also reveals a robust inverse pattern of how masculinity norms relate to economic development compared to norms about women and gender roles.

While economic development goes together with more equal gender roles norms —in line with a positive feedback between economic growth and female empowerment — it is associated with stricter adherence to dominance masculinity norms.

Our cross country results are consistent in the representative subsample of LiTS, as shown in Appendix and discussed in a 4 In order to compare survey answers in nationally representative, face-to-face surveys vs. online samples, we implemented both surveys in six countries.
previous working paper by De Haas et al.
Turning our analysis to individuals, we first discuss individual covariates of adherence to masculinity norms.

Gender is the most quantitatively meaningful predictor of adherence to masculinity norms, with women scoring, on average, 0. standard deviations lower than men on the CMNI. Other covariates such as education, urban vs. rural residence, religion and religiosity are not consistent predictors of masculinity norms.
This stands in sharp contrast with attitudes towards gender roles, for which we observe a clear gradient of more educated, urban, and less religious individuals holding more equal views.
Moreover, the age gradients for masculinity and for gender equality norms are opposite to one another.
While younger men hold more equal views about gender roles, they adhere more to masculinity norms.
We then explore how adherence to masculinity norms predicts individual behaviors and attitudes in three crucial domains.
First, in the economic decision-making realm, we find that individuals who adhere more strongly to masculinity norms supply more labor at the intensive margin and are more competitive.
While such behavior may feed positively into economic growth, it may also sustain gender gaps in labor markets.
After all, gender differences in competitiveness and in the supply of long (and inflexible) working hours are leading explanatory factors of gender pay gaps.

Our results show that gender differences in adherence to masculinity norms explain nearly 15% of the gender gap in competitiveness and 30% of the gender gap in the willingness to supply longer working hours.
By contrast, individual attitudes towards gender roles and gender equality are neither systematically associated with labor supply nor with competitiveness.

Consistent with work linking gender norms to occupational sorting between women and men, we document how dominance masculinity norms contribute to frictions in the labor market by confining men to employment in traditionally masculine sectors.

Our results show that these relationships hold universally in our sample, and illustrate the negative consequences of these frictions for employment in economies with low labor demand in extractive and traditionally male sectors.
Second, we document unambiguously negative associations between dominance masculinity and health and wellbeing.

Key aspects of dominance masculinity—such as emotional restraint, help avoidance, excessive risk-taking, and aggression—are hypothesized to drive gender health gaps, as these behaviors increase risks of suicide, substance abuse, morbidity, and mortality.

Our analysis reveals that stronger adherence to dominance masculinity norms is associated with increased risk-taking—measured through both revealed and stated preferences—and poorer mental health, as measured by the PHQ-4 scale.
Our results suggest that the association between masculinity and the propensity to seek help with mental health issues explains part of the link with depression.
In contrast, norms about gender roles have no consistent association with men’s risk preferences nor their mental health behaviors and outcomes.
Again, these patterns hold in nearly all countries in our sample and explain a substantial fraction of gender gaps.
Gender differences in adherence to masculinity norms explain more than 22% of the gender gap in risk aversion in our sample.

Third, consistent with commentary discussing potential links between masculinity and strongman populism and democratic backsliding, 5 we show that men who adhere more strongly to dominance masculinity norms are, indeed, less supportive of democracy, more supportive of strongman leadership, including by the army, and more opposed to a liberal market economy.

As such, our results also speak to the global gender political divide in support for liberal values. 6 Gender differences in adherence to masculinity norms explain a large fraction of the gender differences in the support for liberal values, with women consistently more pro-democracy and more opposed to strongman leadership across our sample.

The gender gap in adherence to masculinity norms explains as much as 2/3rd of the gender gap in pro-democratic attitudes, 4/5th of the gender gap in support for a strong leader and 3/4th of the gender gap in support for Army rule.

Last, we measure more precisely the contributions of personal preferences and social norms 5 E.g., Blais and Dupuis-Déri; Lombardo et al.; Roose et al. and Washington Post, 20-06-2022. 6 E.g., Financial Times and Wall Street Journal.

Adherence to masculinity norms could reflect: (i) personal preferences and values (i.e., what one likes to do, or how one likes to behave, as a man), which may partly reflect internalized norms; or (ii) social norms, i.e. beliefs about what men should do.
We find that both preferences and social norms matter, but do so to different extents in different domains.
In politics, both preferences and social norms matter, but social norms play a quantitatively larger role.
For competitiveness and risk aversion, preferences and social norms matter, but preferences play a quantitatively larger role.
For depression, only preferences and internalized norms are statistically significant.
Our analysis of masculinity norms—using newly collected and harmonized data from 70 countries across all continents—makes several contributions to economics and social sciences.
First, we expand the cross-cultural measurement of gender norms to masculinity norms.

While existing representative surveys—such as the General Social Survey, World Values Survey, and Demographic and Health Surveys—focus on attitudes about women’s roles in society and gender equality, we answer calls for gender norms measurements focusing explicitly on men.

In doing so, we advance the masculinity norms literature, which has so far relied on small Western samples. 7 Our analysis reveals consistent relationships between dominance masculinity norms and economic, health, and political outcomes across the world.

This broad geographic scope not only provides the first evidence from representative samples (LiTS) but also validates the CMNI scale as a meaningful measure of masculinity norms across cultures and societies.

Our second major contribution is to a rich literature on how social norms shape individual behavior and aggregate outcomes. 8 We demonstrate how dominance masculinity shapes 7 Among 78 masculinity studies in psychology, 65 were conducted in the US, four in Australia, and three in Canada.

While Vandello et al. recently studied precarious manhood beliefs across 62 countries, their sample was limited to college students in an online survey, similar to earlier cross-cultural studies from the 1990s. 8 See Nunn and Alesina and Giuliano for reviews.
decision making and influences socioeconomic, health, and political outcomes.

While the economic literature has focused on norms about women and about women’s and men’s relative positions in society or, more recently, on gender identity, our findings highlight the need to measure and understand the specific role of masculinity norms. 9 In particular, we show that stricter adherence to masculinity norms, and especially the importance given to winning, is a key driver of risk taking and competitive preferences, while gender role norms play no, or an opposite, role and gender identity plays a relatively more marginal role.

Given the documented socio-economic consequences of gender differences in risk and competitiveness preferences, these results illustrate how dominance masculinity norms may hinder further progress in gender equality.
We further advance this literature by contrasting the roles of preferences and social norms.
These distinctions have important implications for policy design.
Policy interventions that may be able to shape the perceptions of social norms through the provision of information may not be so effective at changing preferences and internalized norms.
Lastly, our findings echo concerns about dominance masculinity fueling a political backlash against liberal values and feminism.

In organizations, too, excessively competitive behavior, “masculinity contests”, and harassment and violence against women —arguably all manifestations of dominance masculinity—have been identified as major obstacles to women’s progress and gender equality.
The paper proceeds as follows.
Section 2 provides some background on the definition and measurement of masculinity norms.
Section 3 describes our Global Masculinity Survey.
Section 4 briefly discusses descriptive statistics and global patterns.

Section 5 discusses individuallevel results on the explanatory power of dominance masculinity norms for economic, health, 9 Exceptions include Baranov et al., who examine masculinity norms’ role in Australian socioeconomic outcomes without directly measuring these norms, and Matavelli, who demonstrates how limited gender communication among adolescents creates misperceptions about appropriate male behavior regarding crying and violence.
D’Acunto finds that exposing experimental subjects to gendered behavioral stereotypes increases male risk-taking.
and political outcomes.
Section 6 provides a discussion of the roles of preferences and social norms.
Section 7 concludes.
This section provides background on the definition and measurement of masculinity norms.
Masculinity norms can be defined as the set of attributes and behaviors that society considers appropriate or ideal for men and boys.
These may vary across social domains; for example idealized masculinity in a sports field may encompass different traits than idealized masculinity in a political arena.
However, a key common aspect of masculinity norms is in their relation to social dominance and the justification of male dominance in social hierarchies.

Masculinity norms can therefore be more specifically understood as the configuration of attributes and behaviors that provides legitimacy for the subordination of less respected social groups, which include women as well as men who do not conform to idealized masculinity.
Masculinity norms that serve the legitimation of a male-dominated social order are sometimes labeled as hegemonic or dominance masculinity.

The transmission and persistence of cultural norms operate through the conjoint roles of imitation (either of the same generation, through so-called horizontal transmission, or of older generations, through so-called oblique transmission) and direct inculcation (including for example through the vertical transmission from parents to children).
The processes of transmission and persistence are not independent from the nature of specific norms and their relations to social dominance hierarchies.
The cultural norms and traits of dominant individuals are more likely to be adopted and transmitted for two chief reasons.
First, through an evolutionary-like process, the norms of successful individuals are more likely to be imitated.
Second, dominant individuals in social groups are more effective at enforcing their preferences on others.
As such, masculinity norms are not only held and upheld by men at the top of the social hierarchy, but shared widely in society.

section_name: Measurement: The Conformity to Masculinity Norms Inventory
text: The most widely used measure of masculinity norms in the psychology and public health literature is the Conformity to Masculinity Norms Inventory (hereafter, CMNI).

section_name: Measurement: The Conformity to Masculinity Norms Inventory
text: The CMNI measures the extent to which an individual’s preferences, beliefs, and actions conform to a set of behaviors and values that most distinctively characterize men.

section_name: Measurement: The Conformity to Masculinity Norms Inventory
text: This set of masculinity norms was selected and validated through focus groups, pilots, and clinical studies.

section_name: Measurement: The Conformity to Masculinity Norms Inventory
text: The CMNI includes 22 questions that capture 11 distinct masculinity norms: 10 importance of winning; emotional control; risk-taking; violence; dominance; playboy; self-reliance; primacy of work; power over women; disdain for homosexuals; and pursuit of status. 11 2.3

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: The CMNI asks respondents to rate their degree of agreement with separate masculinity norms based on their “actions, feelings and beliefs”. As such, answers may reflect not just social masculinity norms but also preferences.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: A key distinguishing factor between preferences and social norms, in the framework of e.g.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Bicchieri, consists in conditionality.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Preferences matter irrespective of considerations about what others do or think (e.g. it bothers me to ask 10 The 22 subitems were extracted from 144 original items following a factor analysis. 11 Importance of Winning relates to wanting to be admired and respected, successful/powerful/competitive, performing competently, and being physically adequate.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Emotional Control concerns measures of emotional restriction.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Risk-Taking relates to measures of toughness and adventure.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Violence relates to measures of toughness and violence.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Power Over Women relates to anti-femininity and the subordination of women.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Dominance relates to wanting to be admired and respected, tough, successful/powerful/competitive, and subordinating women.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Playboy relates to adventure, anti-femininity, concealing emotions, and subordinating women.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Self-Reliance relates to disconnecting from others, and in terms of disconnection as measured by the other masculinity scales, this should relate to emotional disconnection.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Primacy of Work relates to being a breadwinner, enduring work like a machine, pursuing success, and experiencing conflict between work and family/school obligations.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Disdain for homosexuals relates to anti-femininity and restricting one’s affectionate behavior with other men.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Pursuit of Status relates to being a breadwinner, admired and respected, successful/powerful/competitive, and performing well ).

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: for help because I do not like talking to other people).

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: In contrast, social norms operate through others’ actions and expectations, or, rather, beliefs about others’ actions and expectations since these are not perfectly observable (e.g. it bothers me to ask for help because I think others may disapprove of my asking for help).

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: The distinction between preferences and social norms does not exclude the possibility that preferences may be shaped by social forces.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: As Postlewaite puts it: “preferences are to some degree socially determined in the sense that agents internalize preferences in some domains that reflect those of the society they inhabit”. Preferences may, in particular, reflect religious indoctrination or parental inculcation, which itself may reflect the persistent influence of salient past historical shocks.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Recent literature has also highlighted how media consumption can change preferences.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: Still, these internalized norms operate through a different channel than social norms, as they should predict behavior independently of expectations about others.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: In this paper, we adopt a version of the CMNI as our main measure of masculinity norms.

section_name: Preferences, Internalized Norms, And Social Norms
text: The online survey includes additional questions on social norms.
This section provides information on our Global Masculinity Survey (GMS) and the measurement of masculinity norms and norms about gender roles.
The GMS is a unique dataset that combines nationally representative face-to-face surveys (LiTS) with large online surveys.

We discuss how this combination enables us to overcome some of the limitations of online surveys in terms of representativeness of the underlying populations, while at the same time leveraging the flexibility of online surveys to hone in on finer measurement of specific dimensions of interest.

The Life in Transition Survey (LiTS) The LiTS is a nationally representative survey of adults’ sociodemographics and political and social attitudes conducted jointly by the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development and the World Bank every four years since 2006. 12 Respondents are drawn randomly, using a two-stage sampling method, with census enumeration areas as primary sampling units 13 and households as secondary sampling units.
It is a repeated cross-section that, at its inception, took place in former Communist Europe and the former USSR, and Germany as a Western Europe comparator country.
It has since expanded to North Africa, the Middle East, and SubSaharan Africa.
Table C in Appendix provides the list of all 44 included countries and national sample sizes.
Section A1.2. provides additional information on survey implementation.
Our goal is to provide global evidence on masculinity norms.

While the strength of LiTS consists in the representativeness of the sample with respect to underlying populations, a potential weakness resides in the limited geographical coverage of the survey – constrained by EBRD’s countries of operation– and the restrictions to the number of questions we could include – constrained by competing organizational interests from the different constituencies involved in such large-scale surveys and respondent fatigue considerations.
To increase the geographic coverage of our survey and enrich our measurement of masculinity norms, we augment the LiTS sample with surveys implemented online.
We primarily targeted continents and regions with limited or no coverage in LiTS, primarily in the Americas, South and South East Asia, and Western Europe.
We first describe this online component and then turn to our measures of masculinity norms. 12 Each round of LiTS is the object of a competitive tendering process.
In the wave used in this paper, IPSOS was the implementing firm. 13 Primary sampling units were drawn randomly, with probability proportional to size.
Online Surveys Online surveys were rolled out in November and December 2024 among 42,761 respondents in 32 countries by the survey company Respondi or its partners.
Respondi targeted participants aged 18-64 years old, aiming to match a representative sample on gender, age, and income.
Nevertheless, a lack of representativeness persists despite quota designs in certain countries, as we discuss in Section 3.1.
Section A1.2. discusses further details on the implementation of the online surveys.
Table C shows the list of countries and sample sizes.
Six countries were included both in LiTS and in the online survey, enabling us to compare responses across representative face-to-face and online surveys.
We refer to the resulting combined LiTS and online samples of 87,359 individuals in 70 countries as the Global Masculinity Survey (GMS).

The online survey is meant as a short and focused survey that includes only the smallest overlap set with LiTS necessary to replicate our analyses and a limited number of additional questions to hone in on the mechanisms underlying the patterns uncovered in the LiTS analysis.

As a result, the survey only includes questions about basic sociodemographics (e.g. age, education, urban status, religion), about gender norms (including masculinity), and the same outcome variables measured in LiTS and presented in De Haas et al. analysis.
We also included one attention check question, and failure to complete it triggered automatic exclusion from the sample.
Descriptive Statistics And Comparison Between LiTS And Online Samples Table C2 presents descriptive statistics on key socio-demographics.
The average respondent is 43 years old, are equally likely to be Catholic or Muslim (23%), have mostly completed at least secondary education (85%), and live in urban centers (72%).
Men and women are similar in these characteristics.

As common in online surveys and despite usual instructions to the survey company to draw a sample that mimics national statistics in terms of gender, age, and income, online samples are not representative of underlying population. 14 Figure B2 compares average covariates in LiTS and the online surveys for countries included in both.
Men, younger individuals, living in urban centers, employed and more educated are overrepresented in the online sample.

However, if anything, respondents in online surveys tend to score lower on the CMNI. We draw on the representativeness of the LiTS component and our initial analysis of the LiTS data circulated as De Haas et al. to ground the validity of our cross-country and individual analyses.
The CMNI-5 The major innovations of LiTS are twofold.
First, we provide the first nationally representative, cross-country evidence using thoroughly-validated masculinity norm questions.
Second, we expand the measures of economic, social, and political behaviors and values that we can link to individual measures of masculinity norms.

To maintain a comprehensive measurement of socioeconomic conditions and social and political attitudes within the constraints of costly face-to-face surveys, we had to limit the number of questions in the LiTS questionnaire.

We provide in Appendix A more background on previous research using the CMNI and on the selection and validation procedures we used to select the questions for LiTS. The resulting CMNI-5 module focuses on five core dimensions of masculinity and measures individuals’ agreements with the following statements:

1. “ Winning is the most important thing ” (Importance of Winning) 14 Lack of representativeness of online samples may be particularly acute in countries with low internet penetration or low internet use.
According to 2023 statistics, the share of population using the internet in the last three months is 67.4% on average, ranging from 36.7% in Sub-Saharan Africa to 97.8% in North America.
Among the largest economies included in the online sample, internet use is high in Brazil (84.2%) and China (77.5%), but very low in India.

Source: World Bank https://databank.worldbank.org/metadataglossary/world-developmentindicators/series/IT.NET.USER.ZS. Representativeness of online sample is also more difficult to achieve in middle and low income countries, where survey firms rarely have their own panels and have to rely on several local partners.

2. “ Sometimes violent action is necessary ” (Violence) 3. “ It bothers me when I have to ask for help ” (Help Avoidance) 4. “ I love it when men are in charge of women ” (Control Over Women) 5. “ It is important to me that people think I am heterosexual ” (Disdain For Homosexuals) Answers were provided on a four-point Likert scale, from 1 (“Strongly disagree”) to 4 (“Strongly agree”), with the possibility of refusing to answer or answering “Don’t know”. We rescaled all responses so a higher score indicates stronger adherence to dominance masculinity norms (that is, more help avoidance, more importance of winning, more justification of violence, more control over women, and a stronger disdain for homosexuals).
We discuss further in Appendix the results of descriptive analyses that validate the high internal consistency of the CMNI scale.
To calculate the CMNI, we take the average across the five domains, creating a score ranging from one to four.
We only average over non-missing answers and create dummy variables that indicate, for each question, whether the respondent provided an answer.
Additional Measures Of Masculinity Norms The online survey includes additional measures of social norms of masculinity.

We elicit the “normative masculinity index”, which measures respondents’ beliefs about the expected behaviors of men in the following core dimensions of masculinity: Help-Avoidance (e.g. “Men should figure out their personal problems on their own without asking others for help”), Importance of Winning (“Men should be aggressive and competitive to get ahead”), Risk-Taking (“It is important for a man to take risks, even if he might get hurt”), Violence (“Men should use violence to get respect if necessary”), and Emotional Control (“Men should act strong even if they feel scared or nervous inside”).
Appendix A1.4. includes the full list of questions, references, and details on how we calculate the overall index.

section_name: Norms About Gender Roles And Gender Equality
text: We also included usual survey questions about gender role norms and attitudes towards women’s social and economic roles and spheres of competence in both components of the GMS. These questions cover various domains, from household labor and childcare allocation, to labor force participation and representation in politics.

section_name: Norms About Gender Roles And Gender Equality
text: The questions were taken from standard questionnaires (e.g. the World Values Survey) and previous rounds of LiTS. The full list of 6 questions is detailed in Appendix A1.

section_name: Norms About Gender Roles And Gender Equality
text: The summary Traditional Gender Role Norms Index (hereafter, TGRI) is the mean of these variables over the six questions, normalized on a 1-4 scale in order to be directly comparable to the CMNI, and coded such that a higher value indicates more unequal views about gender roles and more negative views about women’s ability to be competent business or political leaders.

section_name: Masculinity And Gender Roles Norms Across The World
text: This section provides a brief overview of the global patterns that emerge from the data collection.

section_name: Masculinity And Gender Roles Norms Across The World
text: We focus our analysis on the sample of men, and discuss gender gaps in masculinity norms.

We asked the CMNI-5 questions to all respondents online ( N = 42, 761) but only to male respondents in LiTS ( N = 18, 810) due to lack of pre-validation of the CMNI among female respondents and data collection costs.
Questions included in the TGRI were asked of all respondents in both online and in-person samples of the GMS ( N = 87, 359).

Overall, the CMNI-5 has a mean of 2. and a standard deviation of 0. among the 41,170 male respondents of the GMS. The dimension of the CMNI-5 with the most adherence is Help Avoidance (2.64) and the one with the lowest is Violence (1.71)—see Table C4.
The mean in the LiTS sample is slightly higher compared with the online component, at 2. vs. 2.40.

These patterns are consistent with the normative masculinity index in the online survey: the mean (2.36) and hierarchy of different items are similar ( Help-Avoidance at the top and Violence at the bottom).
The correlation between the CMNI and the normative index is positive but moderate ( ρ = 0.44), hinting that they capture different dimensions.

Figure B3 shows heterogeneity across countries, with some countries, like Korea, above the 45 degree line, suggesting that average levels of adherence to masculinity norms are higher than perceived social norms, and others like Spain below the 45 degree line, suggesting lower average adherence compared to social norms.
We come back to the distinction between the CMNI and the normative index in Section 6.
We report gender gap in the masculinity and gender roles norms measures based on the the online component where questions were asked of both women and men.
Women’s CMNI-5 and TGRI score are 0.4sd lower ( p < 0.001) than men’s.
The largest gender gaps are in the Violence and Disdain For Homosexuals dimensions.
We also document a large (0.56sd) gender gap in the Normative Masculinity Index.
In this section, we briefly discuss variation in adherence to masculinity norms across the world.
The GMS partly draws from online samples, thereby raising concerns about national-level representativeness of the samples.
We discuss in Appendix how we leverage the representativeness of the LiTS component of the GMS to gauge the reliability of inference from online samples.
Figure 2 maps the z-scores of the CMNI, and for comparison the TGRI, across countries.
Adherence to dominance masculinity norms is strongest in Sub-Saharan Africa and South and South East Asia.
The countries with the highest CMNI scores are Benin (3.11), Tunisia (2.90), West Bank & Gaza (2.89), and Algeria (2.80) (all in LiTS).
These regions also include the countries that are the most conservative towards gender equality.

However, as examine in more detail in Figure 3, even within these regions, the countries where attitudes towards gender roles are the most unequal are not those necessarily that also adhere most strongly to dominance masculinity.

Although average attitudes towards women and gender roles are, relatively to the rest of the sample, very unequal in Algeria and West Bank & Gaza, they are more equal in Benin and Tunisia compared to other sub-Saharan African countries, such as Cote d’Ivoire, Ghana, or Nigeria.
India is, by far, the country with the most unequal norms towards gender roles.
However, adherence to masculinity norms in India is comparable to China, or to one of the most highly masculine European country, Latvia.
The co-variation in the CMNI and the TGRI is even more decoupled for western countries and former communist countries of Europe and central Asia.
Western Europe and its offshoots (US, Canada, Australia) clearly stand out from the rest of the world in terms of attitudes towards gender equality.
However, this is not the case for masculinity norms.
The most striking examples are the US and Canada.

While attitudes towards gender roles are much more equal in these countries than in the rest of the sample, and on par with most of western Europe, they stand above the median of the distribution of worldwide adherence to masculinity norms.
The US in particular clearly stands out as the most masculine western country.
The average CMNI score in the US is comparable to Russia, itself among the most masculine in the former socialist bloc.
To explore further how the CMNI and TGRI co-vary across countries, Figure 4 plots the correlation between the CMNI and TGRI indices across countries.
Dominance masculinity norms and norms about gender roles are positively correlated, but the figure reveals considerable heterogeneity across countries.

In the upper right quadrant of countries with high average scores of masculinity and more unequal gender roles norms are most countries in Sub Saharan Africa, Middle East and North Africa, and South and South East Asia.

Most countries in Western Europe, former socialist Europe, and Latin America are found in the lower left quadrant, scoring below average on both CMNI and TGRI. In the upper left quadrant are a handful of countries with low average levels of masculinity but gender unequal norms.
All of these countries are former socialist countries in central Asia and the Caucasus.
In the lower right quadrant, Canada and particularly the US stand out as highly masculine but gender equal.
More broadly, our analysis reveals a striking asymmetric pattern in how masculinity norms and gender equality norms correlate with economic development.
The literature has long highlighted a negative feedback between unequal gender role norms and economic development.

The right panels of Figures 5 and 6 confirm the presence of a strong, negative correlation between economic development, either proxied by GDP per capita (PPP-adjusted) or employment in services, and unequal norms about gender roles. 15 By con We show scatter plots of the relationship between proxies of economic development and either dominance masculinity norms (left) or norms about gender roles (right), partialling out the relationship with the other set of trast, the correlation between either measure of economic development and dominance masculinity norms is, instead, positive.
In the next section, we discuss within-country evidence on the ambivalent economic role of adherence to dominance masculinity norms that may support this aggregate relationship.

This section presents within-country individual-level analyses of the relationship betwe adherence to masculinity norms and outcomes related to economics, health and wellbeing, and political preferences.

We focus our analysis on the sample of men, and discuss how differences in the distributions of adherence to masculinity norms between men and women account for a significant fraction of the gender gaps across these domains.
In what follows, we discuss the results of estimating the following equation:
Y ic =; CMNI ic is i ’ CMNI score; X ic are individual characteristics; and δ c are country fixed effects. 16 We correct for heteroskedasticity and cluster standard errors at the country level.
Age and life stages may be major determinants of adherence to and upholding of dominance masculinity norms.

The strength of masculinity norms overall, as well as the relative importance of particular dimensions of masculinity, may also systematically vary across urban and rural areas because of differences in social structures and contexts (Silva, norms, controlling for continent fixed effects and using population size weights. 16 Table C5 defines the outcome variables and Table C3 presents summary statistics for all outcomes and control variables.
We therefore control for age and urban vs. rural location of the respondent in all specifications.
Education, religion, and religiosity are other important potential correlates of masculinity norms and of our outcomes of interest, especially across our religiously heterogeneous sample.

After our baseline estimates with only age and location as controls, we therefore also show specifications that include education (primary, secondary, tertiary undergraduate level, tertiary graduate level) as well as religious denomination and religiosity in our extended set of controls.

Lastly, to account for non-responses on some of the CMNI dimensions and for potential unobserved heterogeneity across respondents who do not answer specific subitems on the scale, we control in all specifications for a set of dummy variables that indicate whether the respondent answered each specific subdimension.

In order to compare the relative magnitudes of masculinity norms and gender role norms in predicting outcomes of interest, we systematically discuss estimations that regress outcomes on (i) masculinity norms alone; (ii) norms about gender roles alone; and (iii) masculinity norms while controlling for norms about gender roles.
Decomposing Gender Gaps.
Equation 1 is first estimated in the sample of men.

We then examine gender differences by including a Female dummy equals to 1 if the respondent is female, and 0 if male, using the online subsample of the GMS. Next, we assess the extent to which adherence to dominance masculinity and traditional gender role norms account for these observed differences.
We follow the decomposition approach described in Gelbach, which uses the omitted variable bias formula to calculate the exact contribution of each covariate in the gender gap.
That is, it multiplies the raw gender gap in the CMNI or TGRI indices, including the baseline controls as in Equation 1, by their effect on our outcomes of interest with the full set of controls.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: We start by discussing the roles of individual covariates as predictors of adherence to masculinity norms.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: Figure 7 plots coefficient estimates from linear regressions of either the CMNI, or, for comparison, the TGRI index, on a range of demographic and socioeconomic characteristics and country fixed effects.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: Respondent gender is the most quantitatively meaningful covariate in predicting CMNI scores, with women’s 0. of a standard deviation lower than men, on average.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: Age is also a meaningful predictor of adherence to masculinity norms, with younger men scoring significantly more masculine than older men.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: The relationship between age and CMNI is opposite to the relationship between age and TGRI: while younger individuals are more gender equal, they are more masculine.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: Other covariates, such as education, urbanity, religion or even religiosity are not statistical predictors of adherence to masculinity norms.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: Again, this contrasts deeply with attitudes towards gender norms, for which each of these covariates holds a significant and quantitatively large predictive power, with more educated, urban, less religious individuals systematically holding more gender equal norms, and large difference across religions, with Muslims and Catholics holding less gender equal views.

section_name: Correlates of Masculinity And Gender Role Norms
text: In contrast, adherence to masculinity norms transcends education, religion, urban, and rural groups.
Labor supply and occupational choice.

A recent sociological literature describes work as an arena of “masculinity contests”, emphasizing how a strive for dominance and winning may create hostile and excessively competitive work environments that normalize very long working hours. 17 The prediction here is that adherence to masculinity norms correlates positively with labor supply at the intensive margin.

Separately, an economics literature has stressed how 17 Berdahl et al. describe how dominance masculinity norms are pervasive in a wide range of leading companies, such as Uber, Fox News, the Weinstein Company, as well as in Silicon Valley.

gender roles norms and gender identity norms influence occupation and industry choice, with masculinity norms contributing to male specialization in sectors such as agriculture, construction and manufacturing.

Such specialization can become a driver of unemployment when male-dominated industries are displaced or suffer negative economic shocks, implying an overall ambiguous relationship between masculinity norms and employment status.

To assess the relationship between individual adherence to masculinity norms and the supply of labor at the intensive margin, we estimate Equation ( 1 ), using as the dependent variable answers to a question on whether the respondent would like to work more in his current job. 18 Results are displayed in columns 1 (with the baseline set of controls) and 2 (extended controls) of Table 1 (Panel A).
The question on willingness to work more is only asked of individuals that are currently employed, explaining why the number of observations is lower in these specifications.
The results show a positive, robust, and statistically significant (at the 1% level) relationship between individual labor supply at the intensive margin and CMNI scores (Panel A).

Panel C confirms that the relationship between conformity to masculinity norms and labor supply at the intensive margin remains robust and unchanged in magnitude after controlling for norms about gender roles.

The results further show that quantitatively, the relationship between labor supply and CMNI is about two and a half as large as the relationship betwen labor supply and TGRI. The estimates indicate that a one standard deviation increase in the CMNI is associated with a 4% increase in the desire to work more at one’s current job.

Following gender identity theories of occupational choice, columns 3 and 4 of Table 1 show that men who adhere more to dominance masculinity norms are significantly more likely to be employed in a masculine sector (Agriculture, Forestry and Fishing; Mining; Construction; Manufacturing; Transportation and Public Utilities).

While a respondent’s views about gender roles also correlate significantly with being employed in these sectors (Panel B), the association between conformity to masculinity norms and employment in a masculine sector remains 18 Appendix Table C includes details on each variable used in the analysis.
statistically significant when controlling for norms about gender roles (Panel C).
We examine the relationship between adherence to masculinity norms and employment at the extensive margin in columns 5 and 6.
On average, the relationship between a respondent’s CMNI score and employment status is insignificant.
The prediction here is that one would expect a positive coefficient when demand in traditionally masculine sectors is high, and a negative coefficient when that demand is low.
The evidence in Figure B4 is consistent with this.
It shows that in countries that are relatively specialized in the tertiary sector (e.g.
Austria, Singapore), stronger adherence to masculinity norms is associated with a lower probability of being employed at the extensive margin, which is statistically significant at the 1 to 5% level.

By contrast, the association between adherence to masculinity norms and employment is positive and statistically significant in resource rich economies that are specialized in –traditionally masculine– primary and secondary sectors (e.g.
Nigeria, Ghana, Uzbekistan).

The economics literature suggests that a gender gap in competitiveness is an important driver of unequal gender outcomes in education, occupational choice, and labor market earnings. 19 We test the relationship between adherence to dominance masculinity norms and competitiveness using a question that asks respondents “ how competitive [they] consider themselves to be ”, with answers on a 1 to 10 scale.
Answers to this question have been shown to robustly predict actual competitive choices in incentivized tasks.
The results in columns 7 and 8 provide strong evidence that men who adhere more strongly to dominance masculinity norms are more competitive.

Moreover, evidence in Panels B and C show that gender roles norms are, comparatively, marginal drivers of competitiveness and that the relationship between masculinity and competitiveness is robust and stable in magnitude when individual attitudes towards gender roles norms are included as additional covariates.

Result in Figure B4 shows that the positive association between CMNI and competitiveness 19 See Bertrand and Niederle and Vesterlund for reviews and Reuben et al. and Cortés et al. for recent contributions.
holds in most countries in our sample.

Results in Table C6 show that every dimension of the CMNI-5 plays a significant role in explaining economic outcomes, with “Importance of winning” and “Control over women” the most robust and quantitatively meaningful.
Figure B7 presents the results of the decomposition of gender gaps, using the Gelbach approach discussed in Section 5.

We focus primarily on gender gaps in competitiveness and in labor supply at the intensive margins, which have been highlighted as major and persistent drivers of economic inequality between women and men.

Our estimates show that gender difference in adherence to masculinity norms can significantly account for nearly 12% of the gender gap in competitiveness and nearly 29% of the gender gap in intensive labor supply in our sample.
By contrast, gender differences in attitudes towards gender equality plays a weaker role.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Dominance masculinity is often singled out as a driver of excessive risk-taking, emotional restraint, and help avoidance behavior.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Emotional restraint and help avoidance are contributing factors to depression and poor mental health, while risk-taking and help avoidance are associated with lower take up of preventative health measures, including routine doctors’ visits.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: We measure risk-taking in the GMS through both stated and revealed preferences.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: We assess respondents’ self-reported risk preferences with a standard question, which has been shown to correlate positively with risk-taking behavior in incentivized tasks and real-world risk taking. 20 We also gauge revealed risk-taking by asking whether respondents usually wear a seatbelt in the car. 21 20 The question (which is also part of the German Socioeconomic Panel ) asks “ Please rate your willingness to take risks, in general, on a scale from 1 to 10, where 1 means that you are not willing to take risks at all, and 10 means that you are very much willing to take risks. ” The average among men is 5. (s.d.: 2.91) and among women 4. (s.d.: 2.90). 21 We assess seatbelt wearing by whether respondents usually wear a seatbelt, either as a driver (83%), passenger in the front seat (79%), or passenger in the back seat (48%) – see Table C3.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: We assess mental health by including the standard PHQ4 questions—a valid ultra-brief tool for detecting both anxiety and depressive disorders—in the survey.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: These questions ask how often (from 1: never to 5: daily) respondents feel: (i) “anxious, nervous, or worried”, (ii) “very sad”, (iii) “depressed”, and (iv) how often they have “little interest or pleasure doing things”. We build a Depression score index as the sum of the responses to these questions.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: The mean is 2. (s.d.: 1.06).

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Average rates of mental distress are highest in the Middle East and North Africa (highest country-level average in Lebanon: 3.47, followed by Jordan and West Bank & Gaza) and lowest in Central Europe (lowest country-level average in Slovenia: 1.57).

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Finally, in the online component, we assess the willingness to seek help with a mental health professional in case of “personal or emotional problem” as a potential channel explaining the relationship between adherence to dominance masculinity norms and mental health outcomes.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Table 2 shows that adherence to dominance masculinity norms is positively, significantly, and robustly associated with the (normalized) measures of revealed and stated risk-taking (columns 1 to 4) depression (columns 5 and 6), and healthcare avoidance (columns 7 and 8).

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: These associations are quantitatively meaningful.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: A one s.d. increase in the CMNI is associated with a nearly 0. s.d. increase in stated risk taking and a 0. s.d. increase in depressive symptoms.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Panels B and C reveal a much weaker relationship, sometimes insignificant, sometimes reversed, of these outcomes with gender role norms.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Table C7 shows that all dimensions of the CMNI contribute to these results, albeit to different extents.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Importance of winning emerges as the most quantitatively meaningful predictor of risk seeking, while help avoidance and primacy of violence are the most quantitatively meaningful correlates of depression.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Figure B5 displays the estimated coefficients separately for each country in the sample and shows that the positive association between masculinity norms and risk taking, avoidance of medical help and depression are robust and universal patterns.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Gender Gaps In Risk Aversion.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Again, we take the approach described in Section 5. to explore the extent to which masculinity norms can shed light on gender gaps.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Our findings confirms previous findings of a significant gender gap in risk aversion, with women being more risk averse than men, the implications of which for health as well as economic and financial decision-making has been studied in the literature.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Decomposing this gap, results in Figure B7 show that gender differences in adherence to masculinity norms can explain more than 22% of the gender gap in stated risk taking.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: By comparison, gender differences in views about gender equality do not explain any fraction of the gender gap in risk aversion.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Politics The GMS includes a set of questions about individual support for democratic values, support for a market economy, and support for various dimensions of authoritarian leadership, including by the army (see Table C5 for variable descriptions).

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Panel A of Table 3 reveals clear negative relationships between adherence to dominance masculinity and support for liberal political and economic systems.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Columns 1 to 4 show that men who adhere more to dominance masculinity are less supportive of a democratic system and a market economy.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Instead, they are more supportive of strongman leadership and army rule (columns 5 to 8).

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: All these results are statistically significant at the 1% level.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: The magnitudes are large, with a one standard deviation increase in adherence to the CMNI being associated with a 2-3 percentage point (pp) decrease in the support for a democratic regime, a 5-6 pp decrease in the support for a market economy, and a 5 to 8 p.p. increase in support for strongman leadership and army rule.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: When considering the role of gender role norms, either in isolation in Panel B or together with dominance masculinity in Panel C, we confirm previous scholarship and commentary discussing the political role of attitudes towards gender equality.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Our results reveal clearly that both negative attitudes towards gender equality and adherence to dominance masculinity play a role in explaining anti-democratic attitudes and support for strongman leadership.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: This type of leadership often goes hand in hand with performative masculinity, which is displayed by populist leaders or embodied by the military.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Separating the different dimensions of masculinity, Table C8 shows that importance of winning, violence, control over women, and homosexuality avoidance are all important CMNI dimensions that drive weaker support for democracy and a market-based economy as well as stronger support for strongman leadership and army rule.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Figure B6 shows the estimated coefficients separately for each country in the sample.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: The figure clearly shows that the negative association between masculinity norms and liberal political and economic systems and the positive association between masculinity norms and support for strongman leadership are universal.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Furthermore, these patterns are even stronger in more developed countries (i.e., those more specialized in the services sector) in the sample.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Gender Gaps In Liberal Values.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Recent commentary has highlighted increasing gender divides in political values and in particular in support for democratic values. 22 Results in Figure B7 confirm that women are, on average, more pro-democracy, more pro-market, more opposed to strongman leadership and to Army rule compared to men. 23 Results in Figure B7 show that masculinity norms explain a substantial part of this gender political divide.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Gender difference in adherence to dominance masculinity norms explain as much as 2/3d of the gender gap in pro-demcratic attitudes, 4/5th of the gender gap in support for a strong leader and 3/4th of the gender gap in support for Army rule.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Gender difference in attitudes towards gender roles and gender equality are also meaningful contributors to these political gender gaps.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: However, the magnitude of the role played by gender differences in masculinity norms in explaining gender gaps in politics is consistently larger compared with the role of gender equality attitudes. 22 See, for example, https://www.ft.com/content/29fd9b5c-2f35-41bf-9d4c-994db4e12998. 23 The results also show that these gender divides are particularly salient among younger generations.

section_name: Risk-Taking, Health Behaviors, And Mental Health
text: Among younger individuals (40 years old or below), women are 5pp more likely than men to support a democratic regime, and 8pp less likely to support the Army rule, while there is no gender gap among those above 40 years old.
We briefly discuss here the results of several robustness tests underlying the internal and external validity of our findings.
Sample Selection Bias.
First, we leverage the unique, dual nature of the GMS to deal with potential respective limitations of face-to-face and online surveys.
We examine issues related to sample selection bias in online surveys and social desirability bias in face-to-face surveys.

Selection into online samples could bias our results to the extent that the relationships between the CMNI and our outcomes of interest may systematically differ for respondents who select into online panels vs. others.
To assess the extent to which this potential bias influences our results, we systematically discuss the consistency of our results in the nationally representative LiTS sample.
The results are consistent.

The only difference between LiTSand online-based results consists in the relationship between age and CMNI. While we do not find a strong gradient for age as a determinant of the CMNI in LiTS, younger male respondents in the online survey systematically score higher on the CMNI. The age gap is particularly large in the United Kingdom, Netherlands and Germany, where men 40 years old or below adhere more to dominance masculinity by 0.81sd, 0.60sd and 0.48sd.
For a more detailed description of the results, we refer the reader to an initial working paper where we only analyzed the LiTS sample.
Social Desirability Bias.
Social desirability bias may impact reporting of CMNI and TGRI in face-to-face interviews.
We investigate if this is a likely concern by looking at how reporting was affected by the gender of the interviewer.

For the CMNI, female interviewer gender does not significantly predict overall CMNI-5 score, however, men report significantly less agreement to the ‘violence’ sub-item with a female interviewer (Table C9 ).
Meanwhile, for TGRI, female interviewer gender predicts a lower TGRI score by about 0. standard deviations, with the ‘political leaders’ and ‘household chore’ sub-items driving the effect.

Overall, this suggests that social desirability bias is more of a concern with the TGRI. However, all our results are robust if we include interviewer gender as a control in our main analyses, suggesting that social desirability bias is not a concern to the interpretation of our results (Tables C11 - C13 ).
Differentiating From Competitiveness And Risk Aversion.

Another potential criticism of this analysis, particularly in light of a large literature that shows the importance of competitiveness and risk aversion preferences for a wide range of outcomes, is that our masculinity measures may simply capture competitiveness and risk aversion.
We show that this is not the case.
To do so, we reestimate Equation 1 with the competitiveness and risk-taking questions in the set of controls (instead of as outcomes).

Tables C14 - C16 show that, although competitiveness and risk aversion are themselves statistically significant predictors in many cases, the CMNI is still a robust predictor of all the other outcomes of interest.
Moreover, apart from seatbelt use, the coefficients associated with the CMNI are stable in magnitude.
This suggests that the CMNI masculinity measures capture something beyond competitiveness and risk-aversion preferences (Tables C14 - C16 ).
Finally, we investigate whether adherence to dominance masculinity norms is also predictive of outcomes among female respondents.
Using the online survey, which was conducted with both men and women, we reproduce the main analysis for the subsample of women.
The results in Table C17 show that adherence to these norms also predicts economic, health, and political outcomes among women.

Apart from the willingness to work more and being pro-market, all associations between the CMNI and outcomes are slightly attenuated for women compared to men (by approximately 10%), and only one of the coefficients are different statistically (depression, p=0.002).

In this section, we leverage the online component of the GMS to draw a more nuanced picture of the behavioral underpinnings of our results, by attempting to gauge the relative contributions of preferences and social norms.

To understand whether the relationships documented above between the CMNI-5 and behaviors reflect preferences or social norms, we add the measures of normative masculinity (that is, what men think about how men should behave) together with adherence to masculinity norms to Equation 1.

As discussed in Section 3.2., the normative masculinity index comprises of five questions that capture the normative sentiment of individuals along the five behaviors in the CMNI. The interpretation is that, conditional on the masculinity norms, the coefficient on CMNI reflects preferences and internalized norms.
Estimation results are displayed in Table C18.
For labor supply at the intensive margin, the CMNI continues to be a significant predictor.
The CMNI also remains significantly associated with competitiveness, and the degree of association is greater than that of the association between masculinity norms and these outcomes.
For sector of employment, however, we find that controlling for masculinity norms attenuates the association between CMNI and masculine sector.
Finally, we continue to see no association between the CMNI and labor supply at the extensive margin.
Interestingly, the coefficient on Normative Masculinity is significantly negative, albeit small, and may be driven by the phenomenon of retrospective wait unemployment in high income countries.
We see the same pattern across all outcomes related to health.
For depression, the coefficient on CMNI is statistically significant while the coefficient on masculinity norms is neither economically nor statistically significant.
This suggests that the association between masculinity and mental health mostly operates through preferences and internalized norms.
This may represent a significant challenge for male public health interventions.

For political attitudes, we find that masculinity norms are a slightly stronger predictor than preferences with coefficients approximately 50% larger than coefficients on CMNI, although preferences remain significant.
In other words, men who have a stronger preferences for dominance masculinity and who perceive that society should be more masculine oppose liberal values more strongly.

Overall, this analysis advances our understanding of the respective roles of preferences and social norms, which are factors that had so far mostly been either explored in isolation of one another or through measures that confound preferences and social norms.
The results show that preferences and social norms matter to different extents as predictors of competitiveness and risk aversion preferences, work choices, and political attitudes.
If measured alone, the CMNI likely captures both social norms and underlying preferences (which may be internalized norms).
But by including a more careful measure of social norms as a regressor, we also highlight role of preferences.
These results have important implications for policy design.
Recent policy efforts have concentrated on information provision as a policy tool for behavioral change.
Because preferences remain important, they are unlikely to be affected by information provision aimed at corrected misperceptions.
Our results suggest that these efforts, while perhaps effective in some domains, may be less in others and have, overall, small rates of return.
This study has demonstrated how men’s adherence to dominance masculinity norms shapes economic, health, and political outcomes.

Drawing on new data from 70 countries across all continents, we move beyond traditional analyses of gender role norms to reveal how masculinity systematically predicts individual behavior and societal outcomes.

By integrating the Conformity to Masculinity Norms Inventory (CMNI) scale into our survey, we have also created a reliable tool for measuring adherence to dominance masculinity norms across societies.

While prior studies have predominantly focused on selective Western samples, our analysis extends the understanding of dominance masculinity to a much broader context—demonstrating consistent relationships between masculinity norms and a battery of economic, health, and political outcomes.

Our country-level analysis reveals an interesting contrast: while Western nations exhibit more progressive attitudes towards women compared to other countries, they are on par with much less economically advanced economies when it comes to the prevalence of dominance masculinity norms among their male populations.

At the level of individual men, our results indicate that adherence to dominance masculinity norms shapes health and risk-taking behaviors; the supply of labor at the intensive margin and in specific industries; as well as support for strongman political leadership and opposition to liberal values.
Overall, our analysis reveals contrasting results regarding the consequences of adherence to dominance masculinity norms.
While there may be positive effects on economic growth through increased labor supply, the health and political implications appear unambiguously negative.
The strong link we find between men’s adherence to dominance masculinity norms and their support for populist, anti-democratic leadership has important policy implications.

As societies grapple with rising populism and democratic backsliding, our results suggest that understanding the role of dominance masculinity norms could be crucial for preserving democratic institutions.
This is particularly relevant given that populist leaders often deliberately appeal to and reinforce these norms.
Future research could explore several promising directions.

First, investigating how dominance masculinity norms are transmitted within and across generations could inform interventions aimed at addressing potential frictions in the labor market due to the mismatch between masculinity norms and expanding economic opportunity in so-called pink collar jobs.
Second, examining how these norms interact with economic shocks and technological change could help shed light on the drivers of rising support for populism and strongman, authoritarian politics.
Finally, experimental studies could test whether making the costs of dominance masculinity more salient—particularly its health consequences—might reduce men’s adherence to these norms.
